_readme: Refinement surface for the city generator (see spec-meshed-world.md §B/§C). Hand-edit any field to upgrade a city's generated plan without touching code: harbor/sea steer the shoreline, kind steers wall/extent, patron adds a temple POI, races/canonNotes are flavor text surfaced on cards. prov 'canon' = stated by a wiki page or the campaign chronicles directly about this place; prov 'invented' = either a total fabrication (generated inn names, most patrons) or a fact inferred from the Annals' own telling rather than the wiki (per the spec, these still count as invented and get the '✶ per the Annals' telling' banner). A race entry's prov covers only the PRESENCE of that people (canon when a wiki Inhabitants line names them); its optional 'noteProv' separately marks the reasoning in 'note' — set 'noteProv':'invented' when the note explains *why* they are present with detail the sources never give (e.g. Drāmūz: the wiki lists Horatines/Trogmunders but never says why). When 'noteProv' is absent the note inherits the entry's prov. Any future card rendering MUST honor noteProv per-note so an invented note earns its ✶ even on a canon-present race. `biome` ({value,prov,note}) is one of plateau|wooded|arid|frost|riverine|temperate, grounded in the region wiki pages in data/wiki-places.json (Leponnia, Horat, Hoarland, etc.) where possible; 'canon' only when a wiki page states the biome for THIS place directly (its own settlement page, or an unambiguous Description field like Horat's 'semi-arid island'), else 'invented' as a region-grounded best fit. `condition` ({value,prov,note}) is one of thriving|faded|decayed|ruin; 'canon' only where a source says so outright (Epēshu's free-city status, Kolens' 'bustling', Lepon/Sjefenhold/Ion Ephel's destruction), else 'invented' as a kind-plausible default reflecting Leponnia's long post-imperial decline after the Sack of Lepon (745 A.B.) for its lesser old towns/villages. `hinterlandOf` ({name,prov:'annals'}) marks a town as falling within a larger city's canon sphere of influence when the wiki names the district/region but not the specific town (e.g. Epēshu 'exerts much influence over the lands of Pēshunor and Lamor') — prov 'annals' flags this town-level membership as inferred from district-level canon plus this file's own atlas-position data, distinct from a direct wiki statement. Sources: data/city-anchors.json, data/wiki-places.json, scratchpad/epeshu-canon.md, scratchpad/party-sources.md, scratchpad/session-logs.md.

races.name: Patrin
races.note: The city's founding and ruling people.

races.name: Trogmunders
races.note: A sizeable population emigrated here from the Home Islands.

canonNotes.text: Called the Marble City; one of the eight Māgosh, and rules the Marble Gulf.
A crumbling inner wall divides the older, richer inner city from its poorer outer districts.
Harbor splits informally into Wood Quay (old, rotted, smugglers) and Whitestreet (marble-trade, modern).
The Blue Temple, dedicated to Thobrauk, stands at the edge of Wood Quay.
The Epēshīn Forum is the city's civic heart, ringed by the Curia, the Relkhonak, and the Māmban gardens.
Rose to replace Lepon as the Marble Gulf's leading Māgos after the Sack of Lepon (745 A.B.).
The party arrived here in the winter of 1374 A.B. and entered the Thūkīn Amphitheater's Games.

Epēshu's own page states it 'exerts much influence over the lands of Pēshunor and Lamor'; the wiki names no specific towns within those districts, so this town's membership is inferred from its position clustered near Epēshu within Leponnia Kaugrīn, not stated outright.

biome.note: Epēshu's own page: forests and farmlands to its south and east, hills to the north, the White Sea to its west.

condition.note: Epēshu's own page: after the War of the Patrons it 'declared itself a free city and divorced itself from the Empire' — an active, self-governing Māgos with senate, factions, temple, and games underway.

biome.note: Leponnia's coasts are forested; not stated for Sokundo itself.

condition.note: A kind-plausible default for a lesser Leponnia harbor town in the long aftermath of Lepon's fall and the Empire's contraction — patched walls, thinned trade, no canon statement either way.

biome.note: Leponnia's coasts are forested; not stated for Kanae itself.

condition.note: Same reasoning as Sokundo: a lesser Leponnia village in the post-imperial decline, not attested directly.

canonNotes.text: One of the Māgosh of the Leponnian Peninsula, alongside Drāmūz and Epēshu.
Remained loyal to the deposed empress Gimīlahar during the Nepotic Conspiracy, sheltering her forces in secret.
The Road that Walks with the Sun runs through Tamaron on its way from Drāmūz to Cyrikon.

biome.note: Leponnia's coasts are forested; not stated for Tamaron itself.

condition.note: One of the three Leponnian Māgosh, still an active city-state (sheltered Gimīlahar's forces, sits on the Road that Walks with the Sun) — not called 'thriving' outright, but shows none of the marks of decline.

biome.note: Leponnia's coasts are forested; not stated for Mago itself.

condition.note: A lesser Leponnia village in the post-imperial decline; not attested directly.

canonNotes.text: Sits well inland on the Leponnian plateau, out of sight of the sea entirely.

biome.note: Matches this file's own existing note: sits well inland on the Leponnian plateau.

condition.note: A lesser inland Leponnia village; not attested directly.

biome.note: Well inland (sea distPx 36) on Leponnia's dry interior plateau; not stated directly.

condition.note: A lesser inland Leponnia village; not attested directly.

biome.note: Leponnia's coasts are forested; not stated for Āglaz itself.

condition.note: A lesser Leponnia village; not attested directly.

biome.note: Leponnia's coasts are forested; not stated for Mīth Mīrdak itself.

condition.note: A lesser Leponnia village; not attested directly.

biome.note: Well inland (sea distPx 59) on Leponnia's dry interior plateau; not stated directly.

condition.note: A lesser inland Leponnia village; not attested directly.

canonNotes.text: Marks the inland edge of Leponnia Tanrīn, the imperial province of Tamaron's plateau country.

biome.note: This file's own canonNote: marks the inland edge of Leponnia Tanrīn, the plateau country.

condition.note: A minor plateau-edge town, not a Māgos; not attested directly.

biome.note: Well inland (sea distPx 63) on Leponnia's dry interior plateau; not stated directly.

condition.note: A lesser inland Leponnia village; not attested directly.

biome.note: Leponnia's coasts are forested; not stated for Tarap itself.

condition.note: A lesser Leponnia village; not attested directly.

biome.note: Leponnia's coasts are forested; not stated for Thalbaen itself.

condition.note: A lesser Leponnia village; not attested directly.

races.name: Horatines
races.note: Drawn by trade across the nearby Azrāken.

races.name: Trogmunders
races.note: Present as in most White Sea ports.

canonNotes.text: The wealthiest of the eight Māgosh, ringed by tin, gold, and silver mines that fund its public works and fleets.
Its coinage is held to be the strongest in the Wide World; the city is known for bankers and creditors.
The Road that Walks with the Sun begins here, running a thousand leagues to Nub-Nefer.

biome.note: Leponnia's dry interior plateau and the Rhoshkhon mountains lie at the peninsula's south, near Drāmūz's mines; not stated directly for the city.

condition.note: The wealthiest Māgos, ringed by working tin/gold/silver mines and known for its bankers — not called 'thriving' outright, but plainly prosperous, not declined.

biome.note: Inland of Drāmūz's mining country; not stated directly.

condition.note: A lesser Leponnia village; not attested directly.

biome.note: Near-coastal (sea distPx 2) in the Drāmūz/Kardunash cluster; Leponnia's coasts are forested.

condition.note: A lesser Leponnia village; not attested directly.

canonNotes.text: Traditionally marks Leponnia's eastern border, on the line drawn between Kardunash and Nhagi.

biome.note: Harbor town on Leponnia's coast; Leponnia's coasts are forested, not stated directly for Kardunash.

condition.note: A border town, not a Māgos; not attested directly.

biome.note: Inland of the Tamaron coastal cluster; approximated to Leponnia's general coastal-forest texture.

condition.note: A lesser Leponnia village; not attested directly.

races.name: Patrin
races.note: Its people before the sack; some fled to rival Epēshu after.

canonNotes.text: Once a Māgos of the Patrin Empire, seated on the coast and on a nearby island fortress half a mile offshore.
Sacked in 745 A.B. by the empress Gimīlahar, avenging the usurper Tōs-Gither's rebellion; its tallest buildings were burned and Tōs-Gither executed.
Never recovered — deserted over time as its people left for Epēshu and its stones were carried off for other buildings.
General Pebros of Epēshu's Conservative faction was camped among its ruins as of late 1374 A.B.

biome.note: Lepon's own page places it on the Marble Gulf coast; Leponnia's coasts are forested, not stated directly for Lepon.

condition.note: Lepon's own page and this file's own canonNotes: sacked in 745 A.B., 'never recovered,' deserted, its stones carried off.

races.name: Westermen
races.note: Primary inhabitants; seat of the House of Regimen and later King Rennick.

canonNotes.text: The Allhammers' seat, the Ninth House's own telling: capital of Westerland, built about High Hill and the Stony River.
An outer wall rings the old Quarter; a separate inner wall guards the Seat atop High Hill.
The Winehall, headquarters of the Allhammers, stands beside the Stony River.
Site of the Siege of the Seat (1374 A.B.), where Rennick slew Selwin the Last and was proclaimed King of Westerland — the rebellion that broke the party from the Allhammers.

biome.note: Summarch's own page: built around High Hill and the Stony River, which girdles its southern flank.

condition.note: An active royal capital as of 1374 A.B. (Rennick's new reign); not called 'thriving' outright, but functioning and contested rather than declined.

canonNotes.text: Greatest city of the Umbadic Empire; originally founded by Lake-Elves before the Patrin conquered it.
Set on the northern shore of the Five Fingers, rarely troubled by storm, famine, or drought, with waters rich in fish.
Known for its piers.

biome.note: Approximated: Penthelon's own page sets it on the shore of the Five Fingers, a lake system rich with fish — closest available category to a true lake-shore, not a literal river.

condition.note: Called 'the greatest city of the Umbadic Empire,' rarely troubled by storm, famine, or drought; not called 'thriving' outright, but the description is uniformly prosperous.

races.name: Hords
races.note: Hoarland's Mannish people; the region's own page names its largest villages as Sjefen, Kolin, and Hvalstrond — plausibly this Sjefenhold and neighboring Kolens.

canonNotes.text: Frösted Flækes' home village, and namesake of the Sjefenhold Special — destroyed some years past; the tale of its fall is his to tell. Its place on the chart is approximate.
Nearby stood Brandvik the Butcher's old house, which every child in the village believed haunted.

biome.note: Hoarland's own page: cold moors and uplands, snowy forests, gray-white/'hoar' terrain; Sjefenhold (Sjefen) is one of its named villages.

condition.note: This file's own canonNote: 'destroyed some years past.'

canonNotes.text: A bustling riverside city in Diapremon, a country the atlas does not chart — its place here is approximate. Frösted Flækes was a renowned chef at its tavern, the Kolens Connection, famous for knife-throwing tricks between courses.

biome.note: This file's own canonNote calls it 'a bustling riverside city.'

condition.note: This file's own canonNote calls it 'a bustling riverside city.'

river.note: A bustling riverside city — the river is canon; its course through the plan is conjecture.

races.name: Horatines
races.note: Horat's dominant people, held to be especially divergent from their Patrin kin by blood and language.

races.name: Trogmunders
races.note: Make homes on Hordon given the island's relative closeness to the Home Islands.

canonNotes.text: One of the eight Māgosh; the whole of Horat Isle stands under Hordon's dominion or influence.
Competes with Epēshu for dominion over the Marble Gulf.
Alensis City began as a colony of Hordon, and honors it as its imbāgos ('mother-city'), sending tribute every ten years.

Kind ('town') and wall are generator size-class defaults matching how this file already treats fellow Māgosh Drāmūz and Tamaron — not a literal demotion from Hordon's Māgos status, which the wiki attests directly.

biome.note: Horat's own page Description field states outright: 'Semi-arid island across the Azrāken from Patrinor.'

condition.note: An active rival Māgos still collecting tribute from its former colony Alensis; no decline recorded, but 'thriving' itself is not the wiki's wording.

races.name: Goblins
races.note: Co-rule the Kingdom of Goblins and Dwarves from here; the Chief under the Wall resides at Clickerhall.

races.name: Dwarves
races.note: Delved these mountain halls and palaces alongside the Goblins, their friends and co-rulers.

canonNotes.text: Vastest of all the Kingdom of Goblins and Dwarves' works; seat of the Chief under the Wall.
Canon per the After-the-Binding timeline entry 'Founding of the Kingdom of Goblins and Dwarves.'
South of it stands Ernbury, built within a vale rather than carved into the rock, watching over Longdale and the Dale Road.

Kind ('town'), wall (false), and harbor (false) are generator-classification choices, not literal wiki fact — Clickerhall is in truth a delved mountain-hold, not a walled surface town; it has no coast.

biome.note: Mountain Wall's southern reach near Longdale/the Green Sea; approximated milder than the Far North's frost, not stated directly.

condition.note: Mountain Wall's own page calls it 'vastest of all that kingdom's works,' seat of the Chief under the Wall.

canonNotes.text: A colony of the Patrin Empire, established by the mouth of the Tungril, which feeds into the White Sea at the northern edge of Ponbar.
Founded c. 910 A.B., abandoned c. 1060 A.B., per the After-the-Binding timeline.

Its charted position is approximate (edge-clamped in the Far South near the Tungril's mouth, which the atlas does not finely detail); harbor is inferred from its river-mouth founding, not stated outright.

biome.note: Tungril's own page: Ion Ephel was established by the river's mouth.

condition.note: Founded c. 910 A.B., abandoned c. 1060 A.B., per the timeline.

river.note: At the mouth of the Tungril into the White Sea.
